topic.topic: When should an agent discover a new skill rather than compose existing skills?

topic_summary: single_prompt research package for: When should an agent discover a new skill rather than compose existing skills?

primitive_definitions.name: Skill Composition
primitive_definitions.definition: The process of combining existing skills (e.g., API calls, prompts) to accomplish a new task without learning new behavior.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: It is a fundamental operation in all agent architectures; we assume it is cheap but limited by available skills.

primitive_definitions.name: Skill Discovery
primitive_definitions.definition: The process of acquiring a novel skill (e.g., through fine-tuning, prompting optimization, or tool creation) specifically for a new task.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Discovery introduces new capabilities but incurs computational or data cost; it is the alternative to composition.

primitive_definitions.name: Task Performance
primitive_definitions.definition: A metric (e.g., success rate, accuracy, efficiency) quantifying how well the agent completes a given task.
primitive_definitions.why_primitive: Performance is the ultimate objective; the trade-off is about which method maximizes performance given resource constraints.

assumptions.assumption: Skill composition is computationally cheaper than skill discovery.
assumptions.rationale: Composition reuses existing knowledge, whereas discovery requires extra computation (e.g., search, training).
assumptions.possible_failure: If composition requires complex reasoning that fails often, its cost might exceed that of discovery.

assumptions.assumption: Skill discovery yields better performance on tasks that are sufficiently novel relative to existing skills.
assumptions.rationale: Novel tasks may not be decomposable into existing skills, so discovering a tailored skill is more effective.
assumptions.possible_failure: If discovery is poor due to limited data or capability, composition might still be better.

variables.name: topic condition
variables.description: The main condition or task distribution studied by the baseline.

variables.name: proposed mechanism
variables.description: LLM agents face a decision: combine existing skills (composition) or learn a new skill (discovery). Composition is cheaper but may fail on truly novel tasks; discovery requires extra cost but can yield higher performance when existing skills are insufficient. The optimal choice depends on the novelty of the task and the costs of each approach.

variables.name: research quality
variables.description: Whether the proposed question is falsifiable and experimentable.

causal_chain.step: LLM agents face a decision: combine existing skills (composition) or learn a new skill (discovery). Composition is cheaper but may fail on truly novel tasks; discovery requires extra cost but can yield higher performance when existing skills are insufficient. The optimal choice depends on the novelty of the task and the costs of each approach.

mechanism_model.mechanism_summary: LLM agents face a decision: combine existing skills (composition) or learn a new skill (discovery). Composition is cheaper but may fail on truly novel tasks; discovery requires extra cost but can yield higher performance when existing skills are insufficient. The optimal choice depends on the novelty of the task and the costs of each approach.

tensions.statement: Composition is efficient but brittle for novel tasks; discovery is robust but costly. There is no clear rule for when the benefits of discovery outweigh its costs.
tensions.why_it_matters: While skill acquisition and composition have been studied separately, the explicit decision rule for when to discover vs. compose has not been empirically characterized in LLM agents.

candidate_questions.question: Under what conditions should an LLM agent prioritize skill discovery over skill composition?
candidate_questions.expected_contribution: While skill acquisition and composition have been studied separately, the explicit decision rule for when to discover vs. compose has not been empirically characterized in LLM agents.

tension_or_contradiction.statement: Composition is efficient but brittle for novel tasks; discovery is robust but costly. There is no clear rule for when the benefits of discovery outweigh its costs.
tension_or_contradiction.why_it_matters: While skill acquisition and composition have been studied separately, the explicit decision rule for when to discover vs. compose has not been empirically characterized in LLM agents.

research_question.question: Under what conditions should an LLM agent prioritize skill discovery over skill composition?
research_question.expected_contribution: While skill acquisition and composition have been studied separately, the explicit decision rule for when to discover vs. compose has not been empirically characterized in LLM agents.

hypothesis.statement: An agent is more likely to benefit from skill discovery when the task novelty exceeds a threshold τ, where τ depends on the expected cost of composition failure.
hypothesis.predicted_effect: As task novelty increases, the success rate of discovery-based agents will surpass that of composition-only agents, with a crossover point at some novelty level.

minimal_decisive_test.experiment: In a simulated environment with a suite of tasks of varying novelty (measured by similarity to a pretrained skill set), compare two agents: one that always composes existing skills and one that can choose to discover a new skill (with a fixed cost). Measure success rate and resource usage over many episodes.
independent_variables: task novelty (e.g., cosine similarity to nearest existing skill, or number of novel components)
dependent_variables: resource cost (e.g., time, tokens, API calls)
minimal_decisive_test.falsifying_observation: If discovery never achieves higher success than composition on any novel task, or if composition outperforms discovery on all tasks.

supports_hypothesis: Higher task novelty leads to higher success for the discovery agent compared to the composition agent.
The crossover point is consistent across multiple task distributions.
rejects_hypothesis: Discovery reduces success rate on low-novelty tasks due to unnecessary cost.

failure_update_rule.if_failed: If the hypothesis is falsified, revise the assumption that discovery cost is constant; instead model it as task-dependent or consider alternative definitions of novelty.
failure_update_rule.assumption_to_revise: Skill composition is computationally cheaper than skill discovery.
failure_update_rule.next_question: What mechanism explains failure of: Under what conditions should an LLM agent prioritize skill discovery over skill composition?
